title: Cashiers
rationale: Cashiers have significant AI exposure because much of the core job is routine transaction processing that can be automated through self-checkout, computer vision, digital payments, and retail automation systems. The role still requires physical presence and customer interaction, which prevents maximum exposure, but AI and adjacent automation are likely to keep reducing headcount and reshaping the job substantially.

rationale: Management analysts do predominantly digital knowledge work: gathering information, analyzing data, developing recommendations, and producing reports and presentations are all tasks where AI can significantly assist or partially automate. title: Library technicians and assistants
rationale: This job mixes physical, in-person tasks like reshelving materials, staffing service desks, and running programs with routine information work such as cataloging, clerical tasks, and answering basic reference questions. AI can automate or reduce much of the digital and administrative portion, but the physical handling of materials and face-to-face patron support create a meaningful barrier to full automation.

title: Music directors and composers
rationale: AI is increasingly capable of generating, arranging, and editing music, so the composing side of this occupation faces meaningful exposure, especially for routine commercial or background music work done digitally. But a large share of the job—conducting ensembles, directing rehearsals, giving artistic feedback, auditioning performers, and leading live performances—depends on in-person leadership, musical interpretation, and real-time human coordination.

title: Lawyers
rationale: Lawyers do a large share of their core work in fully digital domains—researching law, analyzing facts, drafting documents, and communicating advice—where AI is improving rapidly and can already boost productivity substantially. But important parts of the job still rely on human judgment, client trust, negotiation, strategy, and in-person advocacy in courts and proceedings, which keeps exposure below the maximum.

title: Umpires, referees, and other sports officials
rationale: This job’s core work happens in person, in real time, on the field or court, requiring physical presence, positioning, visual observation, and split-second judgment under pressure. AI may assist at the margins through video replay, training, and administrative tasks, but it is unlikely to replace the core officiating role broadly in the near term.

title: Surveyors
rationale: Surveyors have meaningful exposure because parts of the job are digital and information-heavy: researching records, processing measurement data, creating maps with GIS/CAD, and preparing reports can all be increasingly assisted by AI. But the occupation still depends heavily on fieldwork, physical measurement on site, travel, and licensed legal judgment about boundaries, which limits full automation.

title: Civil engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: This job mixes digital design, drafting, cost estimating, data analysis, and reporting—tasks where AI can significantly boost productivity—with field surveys, site inspections, materials testing, and construction observation that still require physical presence. title: Adult basic and secondary education and ESL teachers
rationale: This job combines significant in-person teaching, motivation, and cultural sensitivity with substantial knowledge-work tasks such as lesson planning, assessment, progress monitoring, and individualized instruction. AI can increasingly handle tutoring, translation, practice exercises, and administrative prep, which could materially raise teacher productivity, but the core role still depends on human classroom management, adaptation, and relationship-building.

title: Graphic designers
rationale: Graphic design is overwhelmingly digital work: creating layouts, images, and visual concepts on software, often entirely from a computer. AI is rapidly improving at image generation, layout iteration, branding concepts, and production tasks, so it can both automate portions of the work and greatly increase each designer’s output, though client interaction, taste, and higher-level creative direction still keep exposure just below the maximum.

title: Environmental engineers
rationale: Environmental engineers do substantial computer-based knowledge work—analyzing data, preparing reports, reviewing permits, and designing pollution-control or water-treatment systems—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity in core parts of the job. But the occupation also depends on site inspections, project monitoring, regulatory accountability, and engineering judgment tied to physical infrastructure, which limits full automation.

title: Special education teachers
rationale: Special education teaching includes substantial knowledge work that AI can assist with, such as drafting IEPs, adapting lessons, preparing materials, tracking progress, and handling documentation. But the core job still depends heavily on in-person instruction, behavioral support, individualized judgment, and close human relationships with students, parents, and staff, which limits full automation.

title: Compensation, benefits, and job analysis specialists
rationale: This occupation is predominantly computer-based knowledge work: researching policies, analyzing compensation data, evaluating job descriptions, preparing reports, and monitoring regulations. AI can substantially automate and accelerate these digital tasks, though human judgment, legal accountability, and stakeholder communication still matter enough to keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Historians
rationale: Historian work is heavily centered on digital and text-based tasks—researching sources, analyzing information, synthesizing findings, translating material, and writing reports or articles—all areas where AI is improving rapidly. Physical archive work, artifact handling, and nuanced interpretation of authenticity and historical significance still require human judgment, but AI is likely to substantially reshape workflows and reduce time needed for much of the core research and writing.

title: Industrial engineers
rationale: Industrial engineers do substantial knowledge work on a computer—analyzing process data, modeling systems, designing improvements, and presenting recommendations—so AI can significantly boost productivity in core tasks. But the job also depends on observing real-world operations, understanding plant- or service-specific constraints, and coordinating implementation with people on site, which limits full automation.

title: Bakers
rationale: Bakers’ core work is physical and hands-on: measuring ingredients, shaping dough, operating ovens, monitoring batches, cleaning equipment, and often serving customers in person. AI may help at the margins with inventory, scheduling, demand forecasting, or recipe standardization, but it does not substantially automate the central manual, on-site tasks of baking.

title: Film and video editors and camera operators
rationale: AI exposure is very high overall because film and video editing is almost entirely digital, and AI can already assist with organizing footage, rough cuts, color/audio cleanup, effects, and content generation. Exposure is somewhat tempered by camera-operator work, which still requires physical presence, equipment handling, and creative on-set judgment, but robotic cameras and workflow automation will still reduce labor needs and reshape the occupation.

title: Political scientists
rationale: This is predominantly digital knowledge work: researching, analyzing survey and policy data, forecasting trends, and writing/presenting findings are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. Human judgment, domain expertise, and credibility still matter for interpreting complex political contexts and shaping research agendas, but the occupation is highly exposed because much of the core workflow happens on a computer.

title: Pest control workers
rationale: This job is dominated by in-person physical work: inspecting buildings, crawling into tight spaces, applying treatments, setting traps, driving to sites, and interacting with customers on location. AI may help with scheduling, recordkeeping, pricing, or pest identification, but it is unlikely to automate the core hands-on fieldwork.
